did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning and transfer learning with CNN architectures (specifically ResNet50, VGG16, and EfficientNet) to classify dermoscopic images for melanoma detection. The methodology involves preprocessing skin lesion images, training models on a dataset from Kaggle (HAM10000), and evaluating performance using metrics like accuracy, precision, recall, and confusion matrices to distinguish between malignant and benign lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of deep learning models (ResNet50, VGG16) for melanoma detection using the HAM10000 dataset. It includes specific methodology sections (Experimental Setup, Methodology), describes the implementation of transfer learning and Grad-CAM, and reports experimental results including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score metrics. Although published in a conference proceedings (IEEE ICTBIG), it contains original technical results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Melanoma Cancer Detection Using Deep Learning
journal: 2025 IEEE 5th International Conference on ICT in Business Industry & Government (ICTBIG)
brief_summary: This study develops a deep learning system for the early detection of melanoma using dermoscopic images to assist dermatologists in clinical decision-making. The researchers utilized transfer learning with pre-trained CNN architectures, specifically ResNet50, VGG16, and EfficientNet, trained on the HAM10000 dataset obtained from Kaggle. The methodology involved preprocessing steps such as image normalization, resizing, and data augmentation to enhance model generalization and robustness. Model performance was evaluated using metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, and confusion matrices, with Grad-CAM employed for explainability. The ResNet50 model demonstrated strong performance, correctly identifying 707 true positive melanoma cases and 713 true negative benign cases, while achieving high training accuracy. The results indicate that CNN-based transfer learning effectively distinguishes between malignant and benign lesions, offering a scalable tool for improving melanoma diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for detection. The study distinguishes melanoma from benign lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The ResNet50 model identified 707 true positive melanoma cases.
The negative class in the binary classification task. The study includes benign lesions as the comparator to melanoma. The ResNet50 model identified 713 true negative benign cases.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of dermoscopic images into 'malignant' (melanoma) or 'benign' classes.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is to 'distinguish between malignant and benign lesions' and 'classify melanoma'. The methodology uses a sigmoid output layer for binary classification where the output probability is thresholded at 0.5 (if probability >= 0.5, lesion is melanoma; else benign). The system categorizes the lesion as benign or melanoma. Evaluation metrics include Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and AUC-ROC.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A dermoscopic image dataset downloaded from the Kaggle skin cancer database, annotated by dermatologists. It contains images of skin lesions classified as benign or malignant.
classes_used: Class 0 (Benign) - benign lesions, i.e. common nevi

use.training: Used for optimization of network parameters and training the CNN models (ResNet50, VGG16) with data augmentation and class balancing.
use.validation: Used for hyperparameter optimization, early stopping, and checking model performance to prevent overfitting.
use.testing: Used for generalization estimation and final evaluation of the models.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and confusion matrix counts.

HAM10000.split: Training (7010 samples), Validation (1502 samples), Testing (1503 samples)

ResNet50.notes: Described as having 'strong performance' and 'better precision than several other pre-trained models tested'. The paper states it 'correctly identifying 707 true positive melanoma cases and 713 true negative benign cases'.

VGG16.notes: Validation accuracy was 'volatile' and 'belonged between 82 to 86%'. Validation loss was 'not stable especially after 30 epoch', indicating overfitting. The model was compared against ResNet50.

EfficientNet.notes: Mentioned in the abstract and summary as one of the architectures used, but no specific quantitative results or metrics are reported for this model in the provided text.

HAM10000.notes: The paper reports that the ResNet50 model achieved high training accuracy (99%) and correctly identified specific counts of true positives and true negatives on the test set. VGG16 showed signs of overfitting with volatile validation accuracy (82-86%). No specific accuracy, precision, recall, or F1-score numerical values are explicitly reported for the final test set evaluation in the provided text chunks, only the confusion matrix counts for ResNet50.

architecture_used.input_size: 224x224 pixels (for ResNet50 and VGG16); 180x180 pixels mentioned in algorithm workflow steps

VGG16.description: 16 weight layers, mostly convolutional layers (3x3) and max-pooling
VGG16.modifications: Upper classification layers eliminated; replaced with Global Average Pooling (GAP), Dense layer (256 units, ReLU) with Dropout (0.5), and Output Dense layer (1 unit, Sigmoid)

ResNet50.description: Residual network of 50 layers with skip connections
ResNet50.modifications: Last two residual blocks fine-tuned; last layers replaced with task-specific dense layers

training_details.learning_rate: Not explicitly reported (text says 'at a learning rate of.' but value is missing)
training_details.freezing_strategy: Convolutional layers frozen initially to maintain pre-trained knowledge; later unfrozen during fine-tuning

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected layers with sigmoid activation
classification_or_output_head.output: Probability of lesion being malignant (binary classification)
classification_or_output_head.threshold: 0.5 (if probability >= 0.5, lesion is melanoma; else benign)

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing: Images were resized to 224x224 pixels for ResNet50 and VGG16 input; one section mentions resizing to 180x180 pixels.
Normalization: Min-max normalization applied to scale pixel values to the range [0,1] to prevent gradient instability.

Data Augmentation: Applied transformations including rotation (±25%), horizontal/vertical flips, zoom in/out (±10%), cropping, and brightness/contrast adjustments to simulate real-world dermoscopic variations.
Class Balancing: Class weighting and augmentation used to balance the dataset due to disparity between malignant and benign samples.
Noise Reduction: Gaussian filtering applied to eliminate unwanted noise without blurring lesion edges.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Rotation (±25%), horizontal and vertical flips, zoom in/out (±10%), and cropping.
Class weighting and augmentation were performed to balance the dataset due to disparity between malignant and benign samples.
Gaussian filtering applied to eliminate unwanted noise without blurring lesion edges.

conclusions: The ResNet50 model demonstrated strong performance in distinguishing between malignant and benign lesions, correctly identifying 707 true positive melanoma cases and 713 true negative benign cases on the test set.
The ResNet50 model achieved a training accuracy of 99% and showed better precision compared to several other pre-trained models tested.
The VGG16 model exhibited signs of overfitting, with volatile validation accuracy between 82% and 86% and unstable validation loss after 30 epochs.

The proposed deep learning system can assist dermatologists by providing automated diagnosis alerts and Grad-CAM heatmaps to highlight suspicious lesion areas, thereby enhancing clinical decision-making.
The study concludes that CNN-based transfer learning is an efficient and scalable method for early melanoma detection, potentially preventing late-stage diagnoses.
The system utilizes a threshold of 0.5 (50% probability) to classify lesions as malignant if the probability is greater than or equal to 0.5, and benign otherwise.
important_remarks: The paper reports a training accuracy of 99% for ResNet50, which suggests potential overfitting, although early stopping was employed.
VGG16 exhibited signs of overfitting with volatile validation accuracy (82-86%) and unstable validation loss after 30 epochs.
The study uses the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have class imbalance; the authors addressed this with data augmentation and class weighting.
No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated solely on the HAM10000 test split.
Specific numerical values for precision, recall, and F1-score on the test set are not explicitly reported in the text, only confusion matrix counts (TP=707, TN=713, FP=37, FN=46) for ResNet50.
The learning rate value is missing from the methodology description ('at a learning rate of.' with no value provided).
There is a discrepancy in input image resizing: the methodology states 224x224 pixels, while the workflow description mentions 180x180 pixels.
The paper is a conference proceeding (IEEE ICTBIG) rather than a peer-reviewed journal article, which may limit the depth of methodological scrutiny.
Clinical relevance is limited by the lack of clinical trials or cooperation with dermatologists for real-world deployment validation, as noted in the future scope.
EfficientNet results are mentioned in the abstract but no specific quantitative metrics are provided for this model in the results section.
